project.title: Phosphoproteomic Changes Induced by Cell-Derived Matrix and Their Effect on Tumor Cell Migration and Cytoskeleton Remodeling
project.projectDescription: Increased fibrotic extracellular matrix (ECM) deposition promotes tumor invasion, the first step of the metastatic cascade. Yet, the underlying mechanisms are poorly understood as conventional studies of tumor cell migration are often performed in 2D culture lacking the compositional and structural complexity of native ECM. Moreover, these studies frequently focus on select candidate pathways potentially overlooking other relevant changes in cell signaling. Here, we combine a cell-derived matrix (CDM) model with phosphotyrosine phosphoproteomic analysis to investigate tumor cell migration on fibrotic ECM relative to standard tissue culture plastic (TCP). Our results suggest that tumor cells cultured on CDMs migrate faster and in a more directional manner than their counterparts on TCP. These changes in migration correlated with decreased cell spreading and increased cell elongation. While the formation of phosphorylated focal adhesion kinase (pFAK)+ adhesion complexes did not vary between TCP and CDMs, time-dependent phosphoproteomic analysis identified that the SRC family kinase LYN may be differentially regulated. Pharmacological inhibition of LYN decreased tumor cell migration and cytoskeletal rearrangement on CDMs but also on TCP suggesting that LYN regulates tumor cell migration on CDMs in combination with other mechanisms. These data highlight how the combination of physicochemically complex in vitro systems with phosphoproteomics can help identify signaling mechanisms by which fibrotic ECM regulates tumor cell migration.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: Samples were solubilized in 8M Urea and passed through 18G needles to dissociate insolubilized materials. Samples in 8M Urea were reduced using 10mM dithiothreitol (DTT) at 60 °C followed by alkylation with 55 mM iodoacetamide (IAA) at room temperature protected from light. Samples were then diluted 10-fold with 100 mM ammonium acetate to bring urea concentration below 1 M. Proteins were then digested using trypsin (20 μg to 1 mg protein) overnight at room temperature and then quenched using acetic acid. Peptide cleanup was performed using a SepPak C18 cartridge and dried to 200 μg/sample aliquots. Dried TMT labeled peptides were resuspended in IP buffer (100mM Tris-HCl, 1% Nonidet P-40, pH=7.4) and incubated overnight at 40C for phospho-tyrosine (pY) enrichment with bead-conjugate of: 24 µg of 4G10 and 6 µg of PT-66 anti-pY antibodies conjugated with Protein G agarose beads in IP buffer (100 mM Tris-HCl, 0.3% Nonidet P-40, pH=7.4) for 6hrs at 40C. Following washes with IP buffer, peptides were eluted in 0.2% TFA (trifluoroacetic acid), and phosphopeptides were further enriched using High-Select Fe-NTA phosphopeptide enrichment kit. Enriched phosphopeptides were loaded on to an analytical column with 50um ID packed with 5µm C-18 beads. Peptides were separated via liquid chromatography coupled to an Orbitrap Exploris 480 mass spectrometry with the following parameters for data-dependent acquisition of MS1 scans: m/z range = 350–2000; resolution = 60,000; AGC target = 3×106; maximum injection time (maxIT)=50 ms. The most abundant ions within a 3 sec cycle time were isolated and fragmented by higher energy collision dissociation (HCD) as follows: resolution= 60,000; AGC target= 1×105; maxIT= 250 ms; isolation width= 0.4 m/z, collisional energy (CE)= 33%, dynamic exclusion = 20 sec. To correct TMT variation in sample loading, 1:1000 (v:v) dilution of pY IP supernatant was analyzed on a Q Exactive Plus mass spectrometer. Peptides were separated on a 140 min LC gradient, where the top 10 abundant ions were isolated and fragmented for MS/MS (Kohale et al Cancer Res. 2021). Two multiplexed runs of samples 4 h and 24 h were conducted with a bridge channel of an equal mixture of samples to allow comparisons between time points.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: Raw spectral data files were processed using Proteome Discoverer v2.5 and peptide-to-spectrum matches (PSM) were analyzed as with percolator q-scores < 0.01, search engine rank =1, ion scores > 15, or with TMT intensities of all combined samples >1500. Variability across TMT channels were normalized using the sample average of mean centered PSMs obtained from a global proteome run performed on the supernatant. Following normalization, phosphosite PSMs were then mean centered and analyzed using the R programming language. Results were then subjected to principal component analysis, hierarchical clustering, co-correlation analysis, over-representation analysis, ratiometric, and statistical analysis.
